serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml bottle of mineral water, as typically sold and consumed in a restaurant or retail setting.

nutrient_source.title: Bottled Water, Mineral (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14555)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mineral water used as the primary nutrient reference. Mineral water is a globally standardised product; values are consistent across approved sources. Sodium value reflects a typical natural mineral water; specific brands may vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Water and Zero-Carbohydrate Beverages — Convention per University of Sydney GI Database methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: Mineral water contains no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic index. By established convention in the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, pure water and zero-carbohydrate beverages are assigned GI = 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Mineral water contains approximately 5 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is clinically negligible and poses no meaningful sodium load for individuals managing hypertension; adequate hydration with mineral water is encouraged.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Mineral water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it the optimal hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes with no impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, beneficial mineral content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mineral water contains no fat, saturated fat, or cholesterol, and its naturally occurring calcium and magnesium content may confer modest cardiovascular benefit; it is a heart-healthy hydration choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mineral water is natural water sourced from underground springs or aquifers, filtered through layers of rock and soil that impart dissolved minerals such as calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, and trace sodium. It undergoes minimal processing — typically only filtration and bottling — and is sold in still or sparkling form. Mineral water contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates, making it nutritionally inert except for its mineral content. It is universally suitable for all dietary patterns including vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, mineral water is the ideal hydration vehicle for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (no glycemic impact), hypertension (negligible sodium), and cardiovascular disease (zero fat and cholesterol). The calcium and magnesium content may support bone health and cardiovascular function. In the context of this record, mineral water (矿泉水 / 광천수) is served at a Korean restaurant in Singapore as a standard beverage accompaniment. It is commonly sold in 500 ml bottles at a price point of SGD 1.80.
common_user_queries: Is mineral water good for diabetics?
What is the GI of mineral water?
Is mineral water safe for high blood pressure?
What minerals are in mineral water?
Is mineral water the same as spring water?
Can I drink mineral water on a diet?

confidence_reason: Mineral water is a globally standardised product with well-established nutrient values in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. All core macros are confirmed zero by the approved source. GI convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages is well-documented. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 databases.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as mineral water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. iron_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero. All vitamin values are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for mineral water. phosphorus_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. zinc_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. selenium_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are naturally zero. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. Sodium value of 5 mg per 100 ml is representative of a typical natural mineral water; specific brand values may range from 1 mg to 30 mg per 100 ml. Calcium 33 mg and magnesium 6 mg per 100 ml are representative values from USDA SR Legacy. The cuisine tag 'Korean' reflects the restaurant context (Mincheng Bibimbap, Funan, Singapore) rather than the origin of the product itself, which is global. Korean mandatory local-language name (korean: 미네랄 워터 / 광천수) is provided per G6 multilingual rule.

improvement_actions_taken: Added zero-macro justification phrases to notes field for all zero core macros (naturally zero, confirmed zero).
Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel positive value) instead of 0 or null, with gi_category 'low', per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Added Korean local name (미네랄 워터, 광천수) to satisfy G6 multilingual rule for Korean cuisine category.
Added Chinese traditional form (礦泉水) to supplement input Chinese simplified (矿泉水).
Documented all sentinel -1 replacements: all vitamin and micronutrient fields set to 0.0 with confirmed zero justification rather than -1, as USDA SR Legacy reports confirmed zero for mineral water.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all language alias strings present verbatim in flat aliases array.
Populated ontology with verified FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes using exact match_type values only.
Ensured all match_type values use only allowed strings: exact, closest_parent, none.
